title: Psalms 108
subtitle: My Heart Is Steadfast, O God

heb.paragraph: "My heart is steadfast (nāk̅ōn), O God; I will sing and make music with all my soul. Awake, harp and lyre! I will awaken the dawn" (vv.1–2). A composite psalm: vv.1–5 = Psalm 57:7–11; vv.6–13 = Psalm 60:5–12. David’s praise among the nations (v.3), God’s love higher than the heavens (v.4).

notes.note: "My heart is steadfast. I will awaken the dawn." The dawn does not wake the worshipper; the worshipper wakes the dawn. Praise precedes light.
"For great is your love, higher than the heavens; your faithfulness reaches to the skies. Be exalted, O God, above the heavens." Cosmic-scale love and faithfulness.

refs.note: Verses 1-5 are taken from Psalm 57, combining earlier materials.
Verses 6-13 reproduce Psalm 60, creating a composite psalm.
Making music with all one's soul echoes the psalm's wholehearted praise.

hist.context: Psalm 108 combines portions of two earlier Davidic psalms (57:7-11 and 60:5-12) into a new composition. This demonstrates how the Psalter was edited and arranged. The combination moves from personal praise to national petition, linking individual devotion with corporate need. Such psalm-construction shows liturgical creativity.

sections.header: Verses 6–13 — “With God We Will Gain the Victory”

heb.paragraph: "With God we will gain the victory (ḥayil), and he will trample down our enemies" (v.13). God claims territories (vv.7–9): Shechem, Sukkoth, Gilead, Manasseh, Ephraim, Judah, Moab, Edom. "Who will bring me to the fortified city?" (v.10). "Give us aid against the enemy, for human help is worthless" (v.12).

notes.note: "Give us aid against the enemy, for human help is worthless. With God we will gain the victory." The thesis: human resources fail; divine resources triumph. Identical to Psalm 60:11–12.

refs.note: David's conquests over Moab, Edom, and Philistia fulfill the oracle's claims.
Obadiah's prophecy of possessing Edom develops the psalm's Edomite conquest theme.
If God is for us, who can be against us? answers the psalm's confidence in divine aid.

hist.context: The divine oracle claiming Moab as a washbasin and Edom as a place to toss sandals (vv.9-10) asserts sovereignty over traditional enemies. The closing confession that 'human help is worthless' (v.12) but 'with God we will gain the victory' (v.13) combines humility about human resources with confidence in divine power.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Be exalted, O God, above the heavens…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI will praise you, Lord, among the nations; I will sing of you among the peoples.ESVI will give thanks to you, O LORD, among the peoples; I will sing praises to you among the nations.NIVSave us and help us with your right hand, that those you love may be delivered.ESVThat your beloved ones may be delivered, give salvation by your right hand and answer me!

rec.text: ""My heart is steadfast. I will awaken the dawn." The dawn does not wake the worshipper; the worshipper wakes the dawn. Praise precedes light."

rows.label: Victory Through God — 108:6–13
rows.text: God claims every territory. "Human help is worthless. With GOD we gain the victory."

hebtext.note: "My heart is steadfast (nāk̅ōn), O God; I will sing and make music with all my soul. Awake, harp and lyre! I will awaken the dawn" (vv.1–2). A composite psalm: vv.1–5 = Psalm 57:7–11; vv.6–13 = Psalm 60:5–12. David’s praise among the nations (v.3), God’s love higher than the heavens (v.4).

"With God we will gain the victory (ḥayil), and he will trample down our enemies" (v.13). God claims territories (vv.7–9): Shechem, Sukkoth, Gilead, Manasseh, Ephraim, Judah, Moab, Edom. "Who will bring me to the fortified city?" (v.10). "Give us aid against the enemy, for human help is worthless" (v.12).

themes.note: Worship: awakening the dawn with praise. Sovereignty: God claims every territory. Composite of Psalms 57 + 60.
